original text:
the eve the biggest lottery jackpot the history mankind, was pondering probability theory (and yes did zoom over the and buy ticket, and this blog becomes somewhat less active you will know why). reminded excellent paper recently came across the role significance tests Charles Lambdin (2012) who has resurrected the arguments against significance tests our fundamental statistical method for hypothesis testing. makes the case that these really represent modern magic, rather than empirical science, and must admit some extent tend agree, and wonder why are still rely the use p-values today, with almost blind acceptance their rightful place the scientific world.
The modern formulation and philosophy hypothesis testing was developed three men between 1915-1933; Ronald Fisher (1890-1962), Jerzey Neyman (1884 -1981) and Egon Pearson (1895-1980). Fisher developed the principles statistical significance testing and p-values, whilst Neyman Pearson took slightly different view and formulated the method use today where compare two competing hypotheses: and alternative hypothesis (Ha), and also developed the notions Type and errors.
all know from our undergraduate studies, with hypothesis testing using p-values two outcomes are possible for statistical test result. Either the test statistic result the critical region, and the test result declared statistically significant the (5%) significance level. this case two logical choices are possible for researcher: they can reject the null hypothesis, accept the possibility that Type error has occurred α). Secondly, the test statistic result outside the critical region (the shaded areas below).
Here the p-value greater than the statistical significance level (α) and either there not enough evidence reject H0, there Type error. should note this not the same finding evidence favour H0. lack evidence against hypothesis not evidence for It, which another common scientific error (see below). Another problem with the that many researchers view accepting the failure the experiment. This view unfortunately rather poor science, accepting rejecting any hypothesis positive result that contributes the knowledge. Even the not refuted have learned something new, and the term ‘failure', should only applied errors experimental design, incorrect initial assumptions. That said, the relative dearth reports negative outcome studies compared positive ones the scientific literature should give some cause for question here.
The Neyman-Pearson approach has attained the status orthodoxy modern health science, but should note that Fisher's inductive inference and Neyman-Pearson's deductive approach are actually philosophically opposed, and many researchers are unaware the philosophical distinctions between them and misinterpret this their reports.
Commonly researchers state and Ha, the type error rate (α) and the p-value determined, and the power for the test statistic computed, (Neyman-Pearson's approach). But then the p-value mistakenly presented the Type error rate and the probability that the null hypothesis true (Fisher's approach), rather than the correct interpretation that represents probability that random sampling would lead difference between sample means large, larger than that observed were true (Neyman-Pearson's approach). This seems subtle difference but technically these are two very different things, and Fisher viewed his p-value objective measure evidence against the null hypothesis, whilst Neyman Pearson did not. They view that this (and particularly single test single study) never "proves" anything.
p-values represent conditional probabilities (conditional the premise that the true) example help understand this difference would that the probability that given nurse female, Prob(female Nurse), around 80% the inverse probability, that given female nurse; Prob(Nurse| female), likely smaller than 5%. Arguments over this misinterpretation p-values continues lead widespread disagreement the interpretation test results.
There also some good evidence that because the way p-values are calculated they actually exaggerate the evidence against the null hypothesis (Berger Selke, 1987; Hubbard Lindsay, 2008) and this represents one the most significant (no pun intended well just bit) criticisms p-values overall measure evidence.
result important (or not).
The give some statistical evidence that the phenomenon are examining likely exists but that about it, and yet should very aware that the ubiquitous p-value does not provide objective, unambiguous measure evidence hypothesis testing. This not new argument and has been argued since the 1930's with some researchers arguing that significance tests really represent modern sorcery rather than science (Bakan, 1966; Lambdin, 2012) and that their counter-intuitive nature frequently leads confusion about the terminology.
Logically has also been noted that p-values fail meet the simple logical condition required measure support, that hypothesis implies hypothesis the converse should expect least much support for there for (Hubbard Lindsay, 2008; Schervish, 1996).
Our problem need practical methods that avoid both dismissing meaningful results, and exaggerating evidence. Bayesian techniques have been suggestion for the replacement significance texting and p-values, but have yet take hold, probably because the simplicity implementing p-values and their widespread perceived objectivity (Thompson, 1998). Overall these concerns should emphasize the importance repeated studies and consideration findings larger context, and ultimately this leads good argument the value meta-analysis.
But the question remains, why are still relying p-values when there are many issues with them, and probably much better techniques?
Beat's and maybe the sooner become Bayesian's the better for science.
Bakan, (1966). The test significance psychological research Psychological Bulletin, 66, 423-437.
Berger, O., Selke, (1987). Testing point null hypothesis: The irreconcilability values and evidence. Journal the American Statistical Association, 82(2,), 112-139.
Hubbard, R., Lindsay, (2008). Why values are not useful measure evidence statistical significance testing Theory Psychology, 18(1), 69-88.
Lambdin, (2012). Significance tests sorcery: Science tests are not. Theory Psychology, 22(67), 67-90.
Schervish, (1996). values: What they are and what they are not. The American Statistician, 50, 203-206.
